I feel misled each time I buy such a title, and in the Lojas ‘Buy’ appears instead of ‘Place *’ * for an indefinite period;

paragraphs.text: This initiative aims to protect consumers’ rights when buying digital goods such as video games. On the current days I can buy securities like “Diablo IV” requiring Activision Blizzard, recently acquired by Microsoft, to maintain a connection to its servers and thereby support the game. If the company so wishes, it will be able to remove players’ access to the title they bought on a variety of platforms such as Steam or Playstation 5 and the latter will be null and void. This theoretical example is real in many titles such as the game video ‘The Crew’ published and developed by Ubisoft was exactly this in 2024. The games were promptly removed from their players’ libraries and any transaction they made in order to have the game was not reimbursed. With this initiative, we do not want companies to get trapped to projects that cause harm or be something retroactive, but we would like any video game distributed in the EU to have an end-of-life plan, i.e. as long as its development is aware that the game may have to offer a self-host experience by the player or even that it has offline functionalities so that the purchase of the product is not effectively null and void. This ‘end-of-life’ plan could only be implemented when the company wished to no longer support its project. I feel misled each time I buy such a title, and in the Lojas ‘Buy’ appears instead of ‘Place *’ * for an indefinite period; The Steam platform is a video game store and therefore also controls the libraries of its users. It would make sense for the practice of distributing shops such as © GOG sp. z o.o. to be forcibly adopted for others such as Steam and EPIC Games in the EU. Any purchase of games and/or software makes the installation files on the device available to the buyer. That is, if the company wants to withdraw access to the acquired digital goods for some reason, it will not be able to do so, as the buyer has in his possession what he bought from his computer or pen-drive. I believe that these measures aim to protect the consumer and combat piracy, I believe that piracy in general is a problem of the service, if buying a game does not make it yourself piracy is not to steal it. This movement is important for the future development of video games a larger industry than cinema and music. We need the help of our representatives in the European Parliament to be heard. The repercussions of such a movement will be well received by the most diverse communities not only in Europe but also in the world, all of whom are in the eyes of this case.
